sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Acute Aortic Dissection is a rare but extremely serious vascular emergency that can be challenging to diagnose in the pre-hospital setting. Occurs when the innermost layer of the aorta tears, resulting in the separation of the vessel layers and creation of a false lumen in the aorta wall. Any abrupt chest, back or abdominal pain described as ripping or tearing in nature and that is associated with evidence of perfusion deficits or focal neurological signs (nerve, spinal cord, or brain) should be considered as an acute aortic dissection until proven otherwise. Chest pain in the presence of an upper limb weakness is aortic dissection until proven otherwise.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Sudden acute chest pain that is excruciating at onset and is often described as sharp, ripping or tearing in quality, but is sometimes tight in nature and can be difficult to distinguish from angina
- Pain is linked to the location of the dissection:
  - Anterior: ascending aorta
  - Neck / jaw: arch of aorta
  - Interscapular: descending aorta
  - Lumbar / abdominal: Subdiaphragmatic
  - Pulse deficits between heart beats
  - Significant systolic BP differences between limbs may be present
  - Pallor, vomiting and diaphoresis
  - Neurological deficits: limb weakness, numbness, tingling, pain or paraplegia
  - Chest pain with associated neurological symptoms
  - Syncope
  - Shock (hypotension, hypovolaemia, tamponade)

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Proximal acute aortic dissection may affect the coronary arteries and mimic acute myocardial infarction
- Differential diagnoses include ACS, pulmonary embolus, spontaneous pneumothorax, pericarditis, stroke, and acute abdomen
- Lethal complications include aortic rupture, cardiac tamponade, severe aortic regurgitation, and end-organ ischaemia
- CONTRAINDICATED when aortic dissection is suspected: aspirin, anticoagulants, thrombolytics, and GTN - these agents are indicated for ACS but are harmful in dissection. If dissection features are present alongside chest pain, do not administer ACS treatments until dissection has been excluded by imaging
- Avoid treatments that may worsen bleeding risk or delay definitive transfer when aortic dissection is strongly suspected

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Position the patient for comfort and minimise exertion and unnecessary movement
- Conduct vital signs, including bilateral BP where this does not delay care
- Consider oxygen only if indicated
- Consider antiemetic if required
- Contact the on-call doctor as soon as practicable and arrange urgent transfer to definitive imaging or surgical care

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Apply cardiac monitoring and defibrillation pads if clinically appropriate
- Establish vascular access
- Consider fluid therapy only if hypotensive, using cautious aliquots while reassessing frequently
- Administer suitable pain relief as soon as practicable
- Avoid unnecessary treatment delays for extensive investigations in the field
- If ACS therapies are being considered, pause and reconsider the diagnosis carefully if dissection features are present

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Increased risk in males, >50 years of age, history of hypertension, cardiac or valvular surgery, cardiac catheterisation, drug use, stress and blunt trauma
- Medical conditions affecting the connective tissue, for example, Marfan’s syndrome
- Increased risk in pregnancy
- Risk of rapid clinical deterioration
- Risk of cardiac arrest
- There is little direct evidence of the effectiveness of TXA for dissecting aortic aneurysm
